passage:
A behavioral economist surveyed adults about their retirement savings habits across four age groups, recording the percentage of respondents who reported regularly contributing to retirement savings.

| Age Group | % Regularly Contributing to Retirement Savings |
|---|---|
| 18–29 | 34 |
| 30–44 | 58 |
| 45–59 | 72 |
| 60–70 | 69 |

The economist hypothesized that retirement savings participation increases with age, but may plateau or decline among those closest to retirement.
question: Which finding most directly supports both parts of the economist's hypothesis?

choices.A: Savings participation rises from 34% (18–29) to 72% (45–59) across the first three age groups, but declines slightly to 69% in the 60–70 group—supporting both the increasing trend and the potential plateau or decline near retirement age.
choices.B: The 60–70 age group has a lower savings rate than the 45–59 group.
choices.C: The 45–59 age group has the highest savings rate at 72%.
choices.D: The savings rate more than doubles from the youngest age group to the 30–44 group.

explanation: The economist's hypothesis has two parts: (1) savings participation increases with age, and (2) it may plateau or decline near retirement. Choice A directly addresses both parts in a single statement: it shows the increase across the first three groups (34% → 58% → 72%) and then identifies the slight decline in the 60–70 group (to 69%), supporting the plateau/decline near retirement. This two-part analysis matches the two-part hypothesis. Choice B addresses only the second part (decline near retirement). Choice C identifies only the peak value without establishing the trend or the decline. Choice D addresses only one step of the increasing trend without mentioning the plateau or decline.
